question_text: Why do liquids always take the shape of their container?

options.label: A
options.text: Because liquid particles are held in a rigid structure

options.label: B
options.text: Because liquid particles can move freely and flow to fill the available space

options.label: C
options.text: Because liquids are less dense than solids

options.label: D
options.text: Because liquid particles are not attracted to each other

correct_answer: Because liquid particles can move freely and flow to fill the available space
explanation: Liquids take the shape of their container because their particles have enough kinetic energy to move around and slide past each other. Unlike solids where particles are fixed in rigid structures, liquid particles can flow freely to fill whatever space is available, while still being held together by intermolecular forces.
